1: Ps 112: Acrostic of the Blessed God-Fearer — Mirror of Psalm 111. Ps 112 uses the same acrostic structure as Ps 111 (22 half-lines, Aleph to Taw) and deliberately mirrors its language: where Ps 111:3 says 'his righteousness endures forever' of YHWH, Ps 112:3,9 applies the same phrase to the righteous man; where Ps 111:4 says 'gracious and merciful is the LORD,' Ps 112:4 says 'gracious, merciful, and righteous' of the generous person. The theological point: those who fear YHWH come to resemble him. 'Blessed is the man who fears the LORD, who greatly delights in his commandments!' (ashre ish yare YHWH bemitzvotav chaphetz meod) — the beatitude opens the psalm. The fear of YHWH (Ps 111:10, the beginning of wisdom) produces delight in his commandments.
2: 'His offspring will be mighty in the land; the generation of the upright will be blessed' — the blessing extends to descendants: 'mighty in the land' (gibor baaretz) — social, physical, economic strength. 'The generation of the upright will be blessed' (dor yesharim yevorach) — the intergenerational effect of covenant faithfulness. This is the covenant promise structure of Exod 20:6 ('showing steadfast love to thousands of those who love me and keep my commandments').
3: 'Wealth and riches are in his house, and his righteousness endures forever' — 'wealth and riches' (hon vaosher) — material prosperity as covenant blessing (Deut 28:1-14 catalogues such blessings). The linkage of material prosperity and righteousness is the Deuteronomic theology, subject to the test cases of Job and Qohelet. 'His righteousness endures forever' (tzidqato omedet laad) — the exact phrase of Ps 111:3 now applied to the righteous person. Paul quotes this in 2 Cor 9:9 to argue that the righteous giver scatters and gives to the poor — and this endures.
4: 'Light dawns in the darkness for the upright; he is gracious, merciful, and righteous' — 'light dawns in the darkness' (zarach bachoshech or layesharim) — the darkness of adversity is where the covenant light appears. 'Gracious, merciful, and righteous' (channun verachum vetzaddiq) — the three divine attributes of Ps 111:4 ('gracious and merciful') now applied to the upright person with 'righteous' added. The blurring of the boundary between divine and human character is the Psalm's theological point: those who fear YHWH take on his attributes.
5: 'It is well with the man who deals generously and lends; who conducts his affairs with justice' — 'deals generously and lends' (tovtov ish chonek umalveh) — the economic ethics of the covenant community: generosity (chanon, showing grace/favor to the poor) and lending without exploitative interest (the OT prohibition on usury within the community: Exod 22:25; Lev 25:35-37; Deut 15:7-11). 'Conducts his affairs with justice' (yekalkel devarav bemishpat) — all economic dealings governed by covenant justice.
6: 'For the righteous will never be moved; he will be remembered forever' — 'never be moved' (lo-yimot leolam) — the covenant security of the righteous: not moved by adversity. 'Remembered forever' (lezecher olam yihyeh) — the permanence of the righteous person's legacy. In the ANE, being 'remembered' after death through one's name and descendants was the primary form of immortality. The righteous person's name endures because YHWH preserves it.
7: 'He is not afraid of bad news; his heart is firm, trusting in the LORD' — 'not afraid of bad news' (mishemu'ah raah lo yira) — the psychological stability of the covenant-trusting person: even when bad reports come, the heart is not destabilized. 'His heart is firm, trusting in the LORD' (nachon libbo batach bYHWH) — the batach (trust, the covenant reliance on YHWH) produces a 'firm' (nachon, established, ready) heart. The correspondence between internal stability and external trust is the Psalm's anthropology.
8: 'His heart is steady; he will not be afraid, until he looks in triumph on his adversaries' — 'steady' (samuch) — the same word as Ps 111:8 ('established/steadfast'), now applied to the human heart. The repetition of 'not afraid' (vv7-8) emphasizes the theme: the double negative reinforces the complete absence of the fear that adversity normally produces. 'Until he looks in triumph on his adversaries' (ad asher yireh betzarav) — the final vindication: the righteous person will live to see the defeat of those who opposed him.
9: 'He has distributed freely; he has given to the poor; his righteousness endures forever; his horn is exalted in honor' — 'distributed freely, given to the poor' (pizar natan laevyonim) — the generous scattering of resources to the needy. Paul quotes this verse in 2 Cor 9:9 as the biblical warrant for generous giving to the Jerusalem collection: the agricultural metaphor of scattering seed (pizar) applied to financial generosity. 'His horn is exalted in honor' (qarno tarum bekhavod) — the horn as the symbol of strength and dignity; exaltation in honor as the divine reward for generosity.
10: 'The wicked man sees it and is angry; he gnashes his teeth and melts away; the desire of the wicked will perish!' — the psalm's final verse pivots to the wicked person's response: rage and dissolution. 'Gnashes his teeth' (shinav yachroq) — the physical expression of frustrated envy. 'Melts away' (venamas) — the wicked person dissolves; his rage has no constructive outlet and consumes him. 'The desire of the wicked will perish' (ta'avat risha'im toved) — the comprehensive reversal: what the wicked person wants (to see the righteous fail) will itself fail. The blessed man of v1 is vindicated by the very contrast the wicked person cannot bear.
